chunk_summaries_after: The panel suggests that challenging behaviour, combined with additional learning needs, contributes to the rise in exclusions. Many local authorities are developing strategies for support and reintegration, but there is a lag in implementing them consistently across all areas. School accountability measures place pressures on schools.

The discussion touched on various topics including a whole-family approach to supporting families with complex needs and training for staff. Funding challenges, particularly due to the introduction of the new Act, are placing pressure on schools. Local authorities have acknowledged that budget constraints may lead to increased exclusions for students with additional learning needs.

Variation in provision between terms and weeks creates challenges for retaining pupils in mainstream schools, potentially leading to exclusions. The new curriculum is seen as an opportunity to improve practices, but getting children back into mainstream education at key stage 4 is more difficult due to being closer to leaving statutory education.

A local authority in Oxfordshire discussed partnerships within schools as part of a model to retain pupils in school settings. David Hopkins mentioned that at least one authority is looking at pupil retention, but he wasn't allowed to say which authority was involved. Capacity issues were also discussed due to insufficient spaces in some year groups. Nick Williams said safeguarding concerns and the need for robust systems are challenges accessing EOTAS provision.

Quality assurance of alternative provision can be challenging due to difficulties in accessing home and private settings. Providers may use their own staff for alternative provision but quality assuring these arrangements is a concern for local authorities. The WLGA is concerned about changes to pupil registration and their implications for data management, funding, and joint working. There are efforts to move away from off-rolling of students who require additional support.

The committee expresses concern about quality assurance in home tuition provided by third-party commissioning arrangements, particularly for pupils with additional needs. David Hopkins acknowledges that it is a concern when home tuition is not commissioned and managed through the local authority. Within the local authority maintained sector, if provision is not being made to meet all pupils' needs, the local authority concerned should be aware of this and deal with it.

Nick Williams emphasized the need for teacher training to include experience working in PRUs and interacting with mainstream schools. Sharon Davies highlighted the limited opportunities for networking and training among providers. Local authorities have met with health boards to discuss partnerships, including those related to supporting EOTAS learners' well-being. A general deficit in child and adolescent mental health services may be contributing to the number of learners ending up in EOTAS provisions.

New teachers lack experience dealing with challenging behaviour. Nick Williams notes that it's not just this reason for people leaving the profession. Schools have implemented policies and use a mentor to ensure consistency across the whole school, including good practice.

Concerns were raised about Dr Ian Johnson's concern regarding an increase in younger children being excluded. He noted that this trend has gone up substantially in recent years and suggested underlying factors influencing exclusions may include behavioural issues, adverse childhood experiences, and school policies. The role of accountability measures in driving exclusions was highlighted as an area for further exploration.
The discussion focused on providing adequate support for pupils at risk of becoming EOTAS. A whole-school approach was mentioned as part of the solution to address this issue.

The committee discussed addressing adverse childhood experiences in education, including teacher training and a proposed new curriculum on health and well-being. Sarah Stone emphasized the importance of young people's voices and skills in understanding emotions. Liz Williams highlighted concerns about awareness and knowledge of children with additional learning needs. Schools need to adapt their practices to support individual children, recognizing that up to 50% may have an additional learning need.

The committee discussed the importance of addressing adverse childhood experiences in various settings, particularly for vulnerable groups who have experienced early trauma. Dr Ian Johnson mentioned that his previous school had become a trauma-informed school and highlighted the need to consider the emotional and mental health well-being needs of this group. The panel emphasized the need to intervene early and prevent challenges from becoming long-term issues.

The panel discussed concerns about self-harm and mental health issues among young people, particularly noting a concern regarding how girls experience this within the school environment and in general, with increases in some cases. Support levels in schools depend strongly on senior management team commitment. Off-rolling pupils with mental health problems is a concern across various settings.

Liz Williams discussed how children with communication difficulties and anxiety issues are often labeled as "naughty" due to a lack of formal diagnosis and understanding from teachers. Dr Ian Johnson highlighted concerns about learners disclosing problems due to fear of not being believed or accepted. The Estyn report notes that there are generally fewer off-rollings from Welsh-medium education to EOTAS, with potential reasons including socio-economic profiles and availability of EOTAS provision.

The discussion centered on delays for young people not in education or training and how these delays exacerbate pre-existing inequalities. Concerns were raised about home schooling's lack of transparency, which makes vulnerable children "hidden or invisible". The panel also discussed individual tuition, including the need to support students reintroduced into mainstream education if needed.

There may be some pluses as well as minuses regarding exclusions and their impact on mental well-being. Support for excluded children is often fragmented across different institutions, with clear responsibility lying with local education authorities in this context. Parents need clear information to support their children effectively.

Dr Ian Johnson highlighted that turning schools into places that consider mental health and emotional well-being first is key to improving the well-being of future generations. He emphasized the importance of a statutory whole-school approach in foregrounding this issue within school ethos. Sarah Stone discussed the link between inequality, adverse childhood experiences, and the opportunity to intervene positively to prevent young people from falling out of school and society.

ISSUE [2]: Many local authorities are developing strategies for support and reintegration.
REASON: While Nick Williams mentions that "many local authorities" have developed a tiered approach to provision, he also notes that there is a lag in implementing these strategies consistently across all areas.

REASON: Lynne Neagle AM is the one who mentions Item 4, and it's about papers to note, not Dr Ian Johnson's emphasis on a statutory whole-school approach.

ISSUE [3]:
Sarah Stone discussed the link between inequality, adverse childhood experiences, and the opportunity to intervene positively to prevent young people from falling out of school and society.
REASON: The transcript says "out of sight" rather than "society", which implies a more literal sense of being out of view rather than abandoning or rejecting societal norms.
chunk_summaries_after: The panel suggests that challenging behaviour and additional learning needs may be contributing factors to the rise in exclusions. Local authorities are developing strategies for support and reintegration, but there is a lag in implementing them consistently across all areas due to insufficient capacity. School accountability measures place pressures on schools.

The discussion highlighted the importance of a whole-family approach in supporting families with complex needs. Training on adverse childhood experiences and trauma-informed practice has been provided to schools. Funding challenges are placing pressure on schools, particularly due to high delegation levels and staff pressures. The introduction of the new Act may exacerbate these pressures.

Variation in provision between terms and weeks creates challenges for retaining pupils in mainstream schools, potentially leading to exclusions. The new curriculum coming in provides an opportunity to agree on a broader range of measures. Getting children back into mainstream education at key stage 4 is more challenging due to the type of learners involved.

A model for retaining pupils in school settings has been tried elsewhere, including Oxfordshire, where a local authority looked at partnerships within schools to keep money in schools and prevent learners being moved around. Capacity issues are caused by not having enough spaces in some cases. Nick Williams said safeguarding is at the forefront of their thinking, but providing additional training to staff in EOTAS provision is a challenge.

Quality assurance of alternative provision can be challenging due to difficulties in accessing home settings. Providers may use their own staff for alternative provision, which is seen as a way to address concerns about quality assurance. The WLGA paper expresses concerns about changes to pupil registration and their implications for the management of data/funding. There are efforts to move away from off-rolling of students, focusing on supporting vulnerable learners.

The committee expresses concern about inconsistency in provision of education for pupils with additional needs outside of PRUs and local authority commissioned tuition. David Hopkins acknowledges that it is a concern when home tuition is provided by third-party commissioning arrangements. Within the local authority maintained sector, if provision is not being made to meet all pupils' needs, the issue may arise.

Nick Williams emphasized the need for teacher training to include experience working in PRUs. Sharon Davies highlighted the limited opportunities for networking and training among providers. Nick Williams mentioned that local authorities have met with health boards to discuss partnerships. A general deficit in child and adolescent mental health services may be contributing to more pupils having additional needs, which could translate into those ending up in EOTAS provisions.

New teachers lack experience dealing with challenging behaviour. Nick Williams notes that it's not just this reason for people leaving the profession. Schools use a mentor to ensure consistency across the whole school, and policies are in place to promote good practice. Janet Finch-Saunders raises concerns about transport issues for EOTAS learners in rural areas, citing large distances as a problem.

Concerns were raised about an increase in younger children being excluded, which has gone up substantially in the past few years. Dr Ian Johnson noted that underlying factors influencing exclusions may include behavioural issues and adverse childhood experiences. The role of accountability measures in driving exclusions was highlighted as an area for further exploration.

The discussion highlighted the importance of a whole-school approach in supporting pupils at risk of becoming EOTAS, which should be statutory provision. This approach aims to prevent exclusion and address adverse childhood experiences by understanding the child's individual needs within the school environment, with consequences for exclusion including an elevated risk of suicide if not addressed.

The committee discussed addressing adverse childhood experiences in education, including teacher training and a proposed new curriculum on health, well-being, mental health, and awareness. Sarah Stone emphasized the importance of young people's voices and skills in understanding emotions. Liz Williams highlighted concerns about participants' lack of awareness and knowledge of children with additional learning needs, noting that up to 50% may have these needs.

The committee discussed the importance of addressing adverse childhood experiences in schools and education in Wales, particularly for vulnerable groups who have experienced early trauma. Dr Ian Johnson mentioned his previous experience as a school governor where he saw the establishment of a trauma-informed approach being widened. The panel emphasized intervening early to prevent lifelong isolation and address the impact of early trauma on individuals' mental health and well-being.

The panel discussed concerns about self-harm and mental health issues among young people, particularly how girls experience this within the school environment and in general, noting quite substantial increases in some cases. Support levels in schools depend on the senior management team's commitment. Off-rolling pupils with mental health problems is a concern, potentially more prevalent in EOTAS or PRU provision.

Liz Williams discussed how children with communication difficulties and anxiety issues are often labelled "naughty" due to a lack of understanding from teachers. The label itself becomes an impediment to getting support. Dr Ian Johnson highlighted concerns about learners feeling uncomfortable disclosing problems due to fear of not being taken seriously. The Estyn report notes that there are generally fewer off-rolling from Welsh-medium education to EOTAS, potentially due to socio-economic profiles and lack of EOTAS provision within a local area.

The discussion centered on delays for young people not in education or training and how these delays exacerbate inequalities and disadvantage. Concerns were raised about home schooling's lack of transparency, which makes vulnerable children "hidden or invisible". The panel also discussed individual tuition, including the need to support students reintroduced into mainstream education if needed.

Support for excluded children is often fragmented across different institutions. The responsibility lies with local education authorities in this context. Parents must know where to go for support themselves and need clear information about their child's rights and entitlements. A national picture of support for these children is lacking.

Dr Ian Johnson emphasized the importance of a statutory whole-school approach in foregrounding mental health and emotional well-being within school ethos. He highlighted that considering mental health and emotional well-being first is key to improving the well-being of future generations. Sarah Stone discussed the link between inequality, adverse childhood experiences, and the opportunity to intervene positively to prevent young people from falling out of school and being socially abandoned.

minicheck_sentence_scores.sentence: The Welsh Government's draft curriculum has received broad support but some stakeholders requested simplification and added depth.
While minor changes will be made, its overall concept remains unchanged, with key steps including workshops with subject specialists and publication by January 2020.
The "what matters" statements will be refined from existing feedback to ensure consistency across schools.
Mental health and well-being are mandatory topics, with specific wording in subordinate legislation.

The curriculum aims to empower teachers to deliver lessons that are engaging and relevant, emphasizing the importance of four enabling purposes: strong leadership, excellent teaching, assessment, and accountability.
An accountability regime is being developed to ensure consistency in progression steps across Wales.

The Welsh government has invested £24 million in supporting teachers with professional learning for curriculum implementation, with a national approach focusing on headteachers assessing individual practitioner needs.
The pioneer schools have reported on implementing the curriculum in practice, drawing out themes and issues, while other schools participate through clusters and networks.
A statutory framework will ensure consistency, and a national conversation on qualification reform will begin in November to define qualifications rather than vice versa.
The Welsh Government's draft curriculum has received broad support, with stakeholders requesting simplification and clarity.
The Minister confirmed that there will be opportunities for refinements and improvements through workshops, QI groups, and an editorial process.

The primary legislation will include statutory provisions for literacy, numeracy, digital competency, relationships and sexuality education, and a framework setting out expectations for progression in each area of learning and experience (AoLE).
The "what matters" statements will be refined based on feedback from children and other sources.
A final version of the curriculum is expected by January 2020, with implementation planned for 2022.
The Minister also discussed plans for a statutory framework to ensure consistency in progression steps and a national approach to professional learning through leadership.

chunk_summaries_before: The Welsh Government's draft curriculum has received 275,000 unique visits and broad support for proposed changes, but some stakeholders request simplification of language, reducing repetition, and adding depth in certain areas.

Kirsty Williams AM has confirmed that while minor changes will be made to the curriculum, its overall concept remains unchanged. Key steps include workshops with subject specialists in October and publication by January 2020.

The "what matters" statements will be refined from existing feedback and included in legislation to ensure consistency across schools. Mental health and well-being will be mandatory topics, with specific wording in subordinate legislation, but a provision for compulsory delivery on the face of the Bill.

Kirsty Williams outlined concerns around religious education and relationships and sexuality education in the White Paper, with mixed views on including multiple faiths and stronger focus on Christianity. A statement will be made on responding to these issues.
The Minister expects to publish a final version of the curriculum in January 2020, allowing schools to plan ahead, and introduce the Bill following Easter recess for implementation in September 2022.

Kirsty Williams highlights that the curriculum itself won't necessarily enhance or detract from equity, but empowering teachers to be flexible can make lessons more engaging and relevant. She emphasizes the importance of four enabling purposes: strong leadership, excellent teaching, assessment, and accountability, as well as child wellbeing. Classroom teachers are crucial to this, which is why implementation has been delayed to ensure they have necessary skills.

Kirsty Williams AM outlined plans for a statutory framework to ensure consistency in progression steps across Wales. The Welsh Government is developing an accountability regime aligned with the new curriculum's aims and objectives. Kirsty denied suggestions of segregation and acknowledged progress made in bridging the gap between Welsh and local government.

The discussion focused on lessons learned from Scotland's curriculum reform, specifically regarding assessment and quality control. Wales is adopting a more measured approach with pioneer schools and external experts feeding in throughout the process, ensuring checks and balances against international experiences. A cluster approach will continue to be important for professional learning support, with peer-to-peer sharing and regional consortia providing differentiated support.

The Welsh government has invested £24 million over two years in supporting teachers with professional learning for curriculum implementation. Each school receives funding to empower headteachers to address specific needs.

Kirsty Williams AM discussed the national approach to professional learning, citing headteachers as best placed to assess individual practitioner needs. The regional consortia work together for consistency in classroom teaching. She also mentioned revisiting coaching and mentoring schemes, schools as learning organisations, and the OECD's support for developing a culture of self-evaluation.

The pioneer schools have reported on implementing the curriculum in practice, drawing out themes and issues, while other schools participate through clusters and networks, despite not all being directly involved in the project.

Kirsty Williams confirmed Wales will continue to attract teachers from other countries and England due to its autonomous teaching system and new curriculum. Professional learning provisions will support teachers adapting to new curricula.

The discussion focused on accountability in the education system, with Kirsty Williams highlighting the importance of distinguishing between assessment for learning and accountability. She stated that categorisation will continue but evolve, while also introducing new teaching and leadership standards as a key part of accountability.
The curriculum must define qualifications, not vice versa, which may require more time for developing aligned qualifications. A national conversation on qualification reform will begin in November.

Kirsty Williams discussed incorporating a Welsh dimension into education, ensuring resources are broad and not confined to specific areas. The new curriculum will contextualise language learning progress depending on school medium. Wales is opting out of PISA's creative thinking tests for 2021 due to preparation for the new curriculum.

Kirsty Williams confirmed that early years professionals were involved in developing the new curriculum and that statutory and non-statutory provision contributed to its development. The curriculum aims to apply pedagogical principles consistently throughout a child's educational journey, rather than having a "false divide" at age seven. The transition from pre-16 to post-16 education will be supported, with further education institutions being part of the curriculum and assessment group. A minimum standard for pupils in pupil referral units and education otherwise than at school settings will be set, covering four purposes and cross-cutting themes, with flexibility to accommodate individual needs.
The committee agreed to note 23 papers together and review them further in a private session afterwards, before resolving to meet in private for the rest of the meeting.

chunk_summaries_after: The Welsh Government's draft curriculum has received 275,000 unique visits and broad support for proposed changes, including strong support for greater autonomy and agency for the profession. Some stakeholders request simplification of language, reducing repetition, and more detail in certain areas to achieve clarity.

Kirsty Williams AM confirmed that minor changes will be made to the curriculum with a focus on refinements and improvements. Key steps include workshops with subject specialists in October, where they will hand over their work to an editorial process, including translation into both English and Welsh. The final publication is expected by January 2020.

The "what matters" statements will be refined and include feedback from children. They are already included in the areas of learning and experience, with specific wording to come in secondary legislation. The need to deliver these statements will be on the face of the Bill, while the wording can be amended through affirmative procedure in subsequent legislation.

Kirsty Williams outlined concerns around religious education and relationships and sexuality education in the White Paper, with some people advocating for a stronger focus on Christianity and others preferring a broader approach to world views and religions. A statement will be made on responding to these issues regarding both RE and RSE.
The Minister expects to publish a final version of the curriculum in January 2020, and introduce the Bill following the Easter recess for implementation in 2022.

Kirsty Williams notes that the curriculum in itself may not enhance or detract from equity. The opportunity for empowering teachers to be flexible could lead to more engaging and relevant lessons. The four enabling objectives around the curriculum include strong leadership, excellent teaching, assessment, accountability, and addressing child wellbeing. Implementation of the curriculum has been delayed to ensure classroom practitioners have necessary skills.

Kirsty Williams AM outlined plans for a statutory framework to ensure consistency in progression steps across Wales. The Welsh Government is developing an accountability regime that does not work against the purposes of the curriculum. Kirsty acknowledged progress made in bridging the gap between Welsh and local government through renewed engagement, but did not provide details on concrete progress.

The discussion focused on lessons learned from Scotland's curriculum reform, specifically regarding assessment and quality control. Wales has taken a planned and staged approach with pioneer schools and external experts feeding in throughout the process, incorporating international experiences. A cluster approach will be important for professional learning support through peer-to-peer sharing. The national network will also provide strategic direction and ongoing support to practitioners.
The Welsh government has invested £24 million over two years in professional learning for teachers. Each school receives funding that empowers headteachers to address their specific needs.

Kirsty Williams AM elaborated on the existing initiative of a national approach to professional learning, citing headteachers as best placed to assess individual practitioner needs. The regional consortia work together to provide a national approach for the first level of engagement and consistency in classroom teaching. She discussed revisiting coaching and mentoring schemes with the regions to improve them.

Wavehill produced an overall report drawing out themes from individual school reflections. The innovation schools programme requires schools to participate through clusters and networks, with each participating school receiving £30,000. There is a risk that some teachers trained in England may not be familiar with the new curriculum.

Kirsty Williams confirmed Wales will continue to attract teachers due to autonomy for professionals and an exciting new curriculum. Teachers trained in other systems can adapt to the Welsh system, and ongoing professional learning provisions will support them throughout their careers. The new assessment proposals received positive feedback, focusing on ongoing assessment supporting children's learning and progression.

The discussion covered accountability in education, distinguishing between assessment for learning and accountability. Kirsty Williams stated that categorisation will continue but has been evolving over the last three years. She highlighted the importance of teaching standards and leadership standards as the first level of accountability. Schools' self-evaluation and Estyn reports provide assurance to the public about school performance.

The curriculum must define qualifications, not vice versa, which may require more time for developing aligned qualifications. Qualifications Wales will begin their national conversation about reform of qualifications as a result of curriculum reform in November.

Kirsty Williams discussed incorporating a Welsh dimension into education with Sian Gwenllian AM, ensuring resources are broad and not confined to specific areas. The new curriculum will contextualise language learning progress depending on school medium. Wales was offered but has yet to decide whether to participate in PISA's creative thinking tests for 2021 due to preparation for the new curriculum.

Kirsty Williams confirmed that early years professionals were involved in developing the new curriculum and that some of it will be delivered in the non-statutory sector. The new curriculum aims to apply pedagogical principles consistently throughout a child's educational journey. FE have been involved in every single AoLE, including post-16 education, to ensure a smooth transition. A minimum standard for pupils in pupil referral units and EOTAS settings will cover literacy, numeracy, digital competency, health and well-being, with flexibility to accommodate individual needs.
The committee agreed to note 23 papers together, before resolving to meet in private for the remainder of the meeting.

chunk_summaries_before: The Welsh Government's draft curriculum has received 275,000 unique visits and broad support for proposed changes, including strong support for greater autonomy and agency for the profession. Some stakeholders request simplification of language, reducing repetition, and more detail in certain areas to achieve clarity.

Kirsty Williams AM confirmed that minor changes will be made to the curriculum with a focus on refinements and improvements. Key steps include workshops with subject specialists in October, where they will hand over their work to an editorial process, including translation into both English and Welsh. The final publication is expected by January 2020.

The "what matters" statements will be refined and include feedback from children. They are already included in the areas of learning and experience, with specific wording to come in secondary legislation. The need to deliver these statements will be on the face of the Bill, while the wording can be amended through affirmative procedure in subsequent legislation.

Kirsty Williams outlined concerns around religious education and relationships and sexuality education in the White Paper, with some people advocating for a stronger focus on Christianity and others preferring a broader approach to world views and religions. A statement will be made on responding to these issues regarding both RE and RSE.
The Minister expects to publish a final version of the curriculum in January 2020, and introduce the Bill following the Easter recess for implementation in 2022.

Kirsty Williams notes that the curriculum in itself may not enhance or detract from equity. The opportunity for empowering teachers to be flexible could lead to more engaging and relevant lessons. The four enabling objectives around the curriculum include strong leadership, excellent teaching, assessment, accountability, and addressing child wellbeing. Implementation of the curriculum has been delayed to ensure classroom practitioners have necessary skills.

Kirsty Williams AM outlined plans for a statutory framework to ensure consistency in progression steps across Wales. The Welsh Government is developing an accountability regime that does not work against the purposes of the curriculum. Kirsty acknowledged progress made in bridging the gap between Welsh and local government through renewed engagement, but did not provide details on concrete progress.

The discussion focused on lessons learned from Scotland's curriculum reform, specifically regarding assessment and quality control. Wales has taken a planned and staged approach with pioneer schools and external experts feeding in throughout the process, incorporating international experiences. A cluster approach will be important for professional learning support through peer-to-peer sharing. The national network will also provide strategic direction and ongoing support to practitioners.
The Welsh government has invested £24 million over two years in professional learning for teachers. Each school receives funding that empowers headteachers to address their specific needs.

Kirsty Williams AM elaborated on the existing initiative of a national approach to professional learning, citing headteachers as best placed to assess individual practitioner needs. The regional consortia work together to provide a national approach for the first level of engagement and consistency in classroom teaching. She discussed revisiting coaching and mentoring schemes with the regions to improve them.

Wavehill produced an overall report drawing out themes from individual school reflections. The innovation schools programme requires schools to participate through clusters and networks, with each participating school receiving £30,000. There is a risk that some teachers trained in England may not be familiar with the new curriculum.

Kirsty Williams confirmed Wales will continue to attract teachers due to autonomy for professionals and an exciting new curriculum. Teachers trained in other systems can adapt to the Welsh system, and ongoing professional learning provisions will support them throughout their careers. The new assessment proposals received positive feedback, focusing on ongoing assessment supporting children's learning and progression.

The discussion covered accountability in education, distinguishing between assessment for learning and accountability. Kirsty Williams stated that categorisation will continue but has been evolving over the last three years. She highlighted the importance of teaching standards and leadership standards as the first level of accountability. Schools' self-evaluation and Estyn reports provide assurance to the public about school performance.

The curriculum must define qualifications, not vice versa, which may require more time for developing aligned qualifications. Qualifications Wales will begin their national conversation about reform of qualifications as a result of curriculum reform in November.

Kirsty Williams discussed incorporating a Welsh dimension into education with Sian Gwenllian AM, ensuring resources are broad and not confined to specific areas. The new curriculum will contextualise language learning progress depending on school medium. Wales was offered but has yet to decide whether to participate in PISA's creative thinking tests for 2021 due to preparation for the new curriculum.

Kirsty Williams confirmed that early years professionals were involved in developing the new curriculum and that some of it will be delivered in the non-statutory sector. The new curriculum aims to apply pedagogical principles consistently throughout a child's educational journey. FE have been involved in every single AoLE, including post-16 education, to ensure a smooth transition. A minimum standard for pupils in pupil referral units and EOTAS settings will cover literacy, numeracy, digital competency, health and well-being, with flexibility to accommodate individual needs.
The committee agreed to note 23 papers together, before resolving to meet in private for the remainder of the meeting.

chunk_summaries_after: The Welsh Government's draft curriculum has received broad support for proposed changes, including strong support for greater autonomy and agency for the profession. Stakeholders have requested simplification of language, reducing repetition, and more detail in certain areas to achieve clarity.

Kirsty Williams AM confirmed that minor changes will be made to the curriculum with a focus on refinements and improvements. Key steps include workshops lasting three days at a time in October, where subject specialists will hand over their work to an editorial process. The final publication is expected to be in January 2020, as confirmed by Claire Bennett.

The primary legislation will also include statutory provisions for "what matters" statements and a framework setting out expectations for progression in each AoLE area, addressing concerns around balance between individual autonomy and national expectation.

The "what matters" statements will be refined based on feedback from children and other sources. Below the areas of learning and experience are the broad concepts that will be included in the legislation. The need to deliver these statements will be on the face of the Bill, while their wording can be amended through affirmative procedure in subsequent legislation.

Kirsty Williams outlined concerns around religious education and relationships and sexuality education in the White Paper. Some people expressed a need for a stronger focus on Christianity in RE, while others preferred a broader approach to world views and religions. She shall be making multiple statements on responding to these issues in the near future regarding both RE and RSE.
The Minister expects schools to be engaging with the draft curriculum in January 2020 and will introduce the Bill following the Easter recess for implementation in 2022.

Kirsty Williams notes that the curriculum in itself may not enhance or detract from equity. The opportunity for empowering teachers to be flexible could lead to more engaging and relevant lessons. The four enabling objectives around the curriculum include strong leadership of our schools with high expectations, excellent teaching, assessment, and accountability, as well as addressing child wellbeing. Implementation of the curriculum has been delayed.

Kirsty Williams AM outlined plans for a statutory framework to ensure consistency in progression steps across Wales. The Welsh Government is developing an accountability regime that aligns with the curriculum's purposes. Significant progress has been made in bridging the gap between Welsh and local government through renewed engagement from WLGA and ADEW, who have expressed support for the new curriculum.

The discussion focused on lessons learned from Scotland's curriculum reform. Wales has taken a planned and staged approach with pioneer schools and external experts feeding in throughout the process. The cluster approach remains important for professional learning support through peer-to-peer sharing. The national network will continue to bring together practitioners, experts, and colleagues across the middle tier to provide strategic direction and ongoing support.

The Welsh government made the largest investment in professional learning since devolution, a hard-won resource, for teachers. This money was made available to each school, empowering headteachers to address their specific needs.

Kirsty Williams AM described a national approach to professional learning in terms of leadership, with regional consortia working together to implement a curriculum for the first level of engagement and provide consistency for classroom teachers and teaching assistants. She discussed revisiting coaching and mentoring schemes with the regions to improve them. The innovation schools will work on refining content, assessment, and accountability issues.

Wavehill produced an overall report drawing out themes from individual school reflections. Each participating school in the innovation schools programme receives £30,000. The innovation schools met to discuss how they would practically implement the new curriculum. There is a risk that some teachers trained in England may not be familiar with the new curriculum.

Wales will continue to attract teachers due to autonomy for professionals and an exciting new curriculum. Teachers trained in other systems can still come to teach in Wales, as there is ongoing professional learning provision to support them. The new assessment proposals generally received positive feedback, with some suggestions for clearer definitions of formative and summative assessments.

The discussion covered accountability in education, emphasizing the need to make clear the distinction between assessment for learning and accountability to avoid negative outcomes. Categorisation will continue but has been evolving since coming into office. Schools' self-evaluation, including a school development plan, and Estyn reports provide assurance to the public about school performance.
The curriculum must define qualifications, not vice versa. Qualifications Wales will begin their national conversation about reform of qualifications in November.

Kirsty Williams discussed incorporating a Welsh dimension into education with Sian Gwenllian AM, ensuring resources are broad and not confined to specific areas. The continuum for language learning progress will be contextualised depending on the setting where a child is being taught. Wales has yet to decide whether to participate in PISA's creative thinking tests for 2021 due to preparation for the new curriculum.

Kirsty Williams confirmed that early years professionals were involved in developing the new curriculum and that some of it will be delivered in the non-statutory sector. The new curriculum aims to extend pedagogical principles further into children's educational journey. FE have been involved in every single AoLE, including post-16 education. A minimum standard for pupils in pupil referral units and EOTAS settings will cover the four purposes and cross-cutting themes of literacy, numeracy, digital competency, and health and well-being.
The committee agreed to note 23 papers together, before resolving to meet in private for the rest of the meeting.

After reviewing the summary against the transcript section, I've identified the following issues:

ISSUE [1]: The discussion covered accountability in education, emphasizing the need to make clear the distinction between assessment for learning and accountability to avoid negative outcomes.
REASON: This sentence does not accurately capture the complexity of Kirsty Williams AM's explanation. In the transcript, she mentions that making this distinction is "absolutely clear" in the system, but then cautions about the potential for negative unintended consequences if not managed properly.

ISSUE [2]: Categorisation will continue but has been evolving since coming into office.
REASON: Kirsty Williams AM does mention that categorisation was introduced before her party came into office and mentions adapting it "since coming into office", but there is no explicit statement about categorisation continuing to evolve as a result.

ISSUE [3]: Schools' self-evaluation, including a school development plan, and Estyn reports provide assurance to the public about school performance.
REASON: This sentence suggests that schools' self-evaluations and Estyn reports are separate sources of public assurance. However, in the transcript, Kirsty Williams AM implies that the self-evaluation process is more like an "early step" that informs the ultimate assessment provided by Estyn.

ISSUE [4]: The discussion highlighted the importance of teachers and leaders being accountable for their professionalism.
REASON: This sentence does not capture the nuance presented in the transcript. Kirsty Williams AM emphasizes that "the first level of accountability in that system has to be the professionalism of an individual member of staff" but then goes on to describe the teaching and leadership standards as part of a broader framework.

ISSUE [5]: Estyn reports provide public confidence ultimately.
REASON: This sentence misrepresents the transcript. Kirsty Williams AM states that "the ultimate...system that we're going to get to and the ultimate arbiter and the part of the system that provides public assurance and public confidence" is indeed Estyn, but adds "but it starts with our teaching standards and the professionalism of individual members of staff".

After reviewing the summary against the transcript section, I have identified the following errors:

ISSUE [1]: The committee agreed to note 23 papers together, before resolving to meet in private for the rest of the meeting.
REASON: Lynne Neagle AM suggested noting all papers together, but she did not say it was a decision that had already been made; she asked if Members were content with the suggestion.
chunk_summaries_after: The Welsh Government's draft curriculum has received broad support for proposed changes. Stakeholders have requested simplification of language and reducing repetition in certain areas. Many also want to see more detail added to achieve clarity.

Kirsty Williams AM confirmed that there will be opportunities for refinements and improvements with a focus on doing things better, explaining things better, and simplifying where possible. Key steps include workshops in October, followed by QI groups completing their work by November and handing over refinements to an editorial process. The final publication is expected to be in January 2020.

The primary legislation will also include statutory provisions for a number of areas including literacy, numeracy, digital competency, relationships and sexuality education, "what matters" statements, and a framework setting out expectations for progression in each AoLE area.

The "what matters" statements will not be added to existing content but rather refined based on feedback from children and other sources. Below the areas of learning and experience are the broad concepts that will be legislated for. The need to deliver these statements will be on the face of the Bill, while their wording will be in secondary legislation.

Kirsty Williams discussed various responses to religious education and relationships and sexuality education in the White Paper, including both criticisms and suggestions for improvement. Some stakeholders expressed a need to modernise RE and ensure it prepares learners for a diverse society. She shall be making a statement on how to respond to these issues in the near future regarding both RE and RSE.
The Minister expects a final version of the curriculum in January 2020. The Bill will be introduced following the Easter recess for implementation in 2022.

Kirsty Williams notes that the curriculum on its own can play a part in equity. Empowering teachers to be flexible could lead to more engaging and relevant lessons. The four enabling purposes include strong leadership of our schools with high expectations, excellent teaching, assessment, and accountability, as well as addressing child wellbeing. Implementation has been delayed due to a decision to take the difficult step to delay it.

Kirsty Williams AM outlined plans for a statutory framework to ensure consistency in progression steps. The Welsh Government is developing an accountability regime that aligns with the aims and objectives of the new curriculum. Significant progress has been made in bridging the gap between Welsh and local government through renewed engagement from WLGA and ADEW, who have expressed support for what's going on.

The discussion covered lessons learned from several countries' curriculum reforms, including Scotland's. Wales' strategy using pioneers, external experts, cluster groups, and regional consortia has been "more measured and planned against a planned timescale". The cluster approach remains important for professional learning support through peer-to-peer sharing. A national network will provide strategic direction and ongoing support.

The Welsh government made a hard-won resource investment of £24 million over two years for professional learning since devolution. This money is being made available to each and every school, empowering headteachers to think about the professional needs of their school.

Kirsty Williams AM described a national approach to professional learning in terms of leadership, with regional consortia working together to implement a curriculum for the first level of engagement and provide consistency for classroom teachers and teaching assistants. She discussed revisiting coaching and mentoring schemes with the regions to improve them. The 16 innovation schools were selected from 170 pioneer schools, invited to apply for the next phase.

Wavehill drew together individual school reports into an overall report. Each school that participates in the innovation schools programme gets £30,000. The innovation schools met to discuss how they would practically implement the new curriculum. Schools are working with regions on readiness to deliver the curriculum, and some teachers trained in England may not be familiar with it.

Kirsty Williams thinks Wales will be a really attractive place to be a teacher because of autonomy and an exciting new curriculum. Teachers trained in other systems can still come to teach in Wales, but there may be pedagogical concepts they are not familiar with. The new assessment proposals received positive feedback, with suggestions for clearer definitions of formative and summative assessments.

The discussion covered accountability in education, emphasizing that making a clear distinction between assessment for learning and accountability is crucial to avoid negative outcomes. Categorisation was introduced before the current party came into office and has been adapted since then. Schools' self-evaluation, including a school development plan, informs Estyn reports, which ultimately provide public assurance and confidence about school performance. The first level of accountability lies in teachers' and leaders' professionalism, supported by teaching and leadership standards.

The outcomes of prioritisation will be published through each school's website as a development plan with priorities. Qualifications Wales agree that qualifications must arise out of the curriculum and not dictate it. They will begin their national conversation about reform of qualifications in November. The new curriculum will consider reflection on progress, reviews of mental health and well-being, and approaches to these areas.

Kirsty Williams discussed incorporating a Welsh dimension into education with Sian Gwenllian AM, ensuring resources are broad and not confined to specific areas. The continuum for language learning progress will be contextualised depending on the setting where a child is being taught. Wales has opted out of PISA's creative thinking tests for 2020 due to preparation for the new curriculum.

Kirsty Williams confirmed that early years professionals have been involved in the curriculum to date and some of it will be delivered in non-statutory provision. FE have been part of this process throughout, with involvement in AoLEs. A minimum standard for pupils in pupil referral units and EOTAS settings will cover the four purposes and cross-cutting themes of literacy, numeracy, digital competency, and health and well-being.

Lynne Neagle AM suggested noting all 23 papers together before resolving to meet in private for the remainder of the meeting. She proposed this in accordance with Standing Order 17.42 and asked Members if they were content.
